meta.manuscript_location: Shrine of the Book, Israel Museum, Jerusalem

preamble.summary: Isaiah 6, the throne-room vision and prophetic commissioning, is one of the most theologically important chapters in the book. 1QIsaiah-a preserves this chapter in good condition with mostly minor variants. The trisagion ('Holy, holy, holy') in verse 3 reads identically in both traditions.
preamble.notable_variants: The trisagion (v. 3) is identical. Verse 3 shows 1QIsaiah-a writing the divine name YHWH in paleo-Hebrew script (the archaic Hebrew alphabet), which is a characteristic feature of 1QIsaiah-a throughout — the scribe switches to the old script for the Tetragrammaton. Verse 13 has a notable variant where 1QIsaiah-a appears to have a slightly different reading of the 'holy seed' passage.
preamble.scroll_condition: Good condition. Fully legible. The divine name appears in paleo-Hebrew characters.
preamble.column_reference: Columns V–VI of 1QIsaiah-a contain Isaiah chapter 6.

variant_notes: Plene spelling with waw. The throne vision opens identically: 'In the year that King Uzziah died.'
Identical consonantal text. The six-winged seraphim standing above the throne are described identically.

verses.variant_rendering: Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of Hosts
verses.mt_rendering: Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of Hosts
variant_notes: The trisagion — the threefold 'Holy' — reads identically in 1QIsaiah-a and the MT. This is significant because it confirms the antiquity and stability of this liturgical formula, which became central to both Jewish and Christian worship.

1QIsaiah-a writes the divine name YHWH in paleo-Hebrew (archaic) script here, as it does throughout the scroll. This practice reflects the special reverence accorded to the divine name in the Second Temple period — the scribe switches from the standard Aramaic square script to the old Hebrew script specifically for the Tetragrammaton.
The phrase 'the whole earth is full of his glory' (melo kol ha'arets kevodo) is identical in both traditions.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
Identical consonantal text. Isaiah's response of terror — 'Woe to me, I am undone/silenced!' — reads the same. The verb nidmeti can mean 'destroyed,' 'silenced,' or 'cut off.'
Identical consonantal text. The seraph takes a ritspah ('burning coal, glowing stone') from the altar with tongs. Both traditions agree on this purification instrument.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.

verses.dss_reading: את מי אשלח ומי ילך לנו
verses.variant_rendering: Whom shall I send, and who will go for us?
verses.mt_rendering: Whom shall I send, and who will go for us?
variant_notes: The consonantal text is identical. The divine plural 'for us' (lanu) — one of the most discussed phrases in Isaiah — reads the same in both traditions. Whether this reflects a divine council, a trinitarian hint, or a plural of majesty, 1QIsaiah-a provides no variant to resolve the question.

verses.variant_rendering: Hear indeed, but do not understand
verses.mt_rendering: Hear indeed, but do not understand
variant_notes: The hardening commission reads identically. This passage, quoted by Jesus in all four Gospels (Matthew 13:14-15, Mark 4:12, Luke 8:10, John 12:40) and by Paul in Acts 28:26-27, is textually stable across traditions.

verses.variant_rendering: Make the heart of this people dull
verses.mt_rendering: Make the heart of this people dull
variant_notes: Identical reading. The command to 'fatten' (hashmen) the people's heart — to render them spiritually insensible — reads the same. The three-part structure (heart/ears/eyes) is preserved.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.

verses.variant_rendering: the holy seed is its stump
verses.mt_rendering: the holy seed is its stump
variant_notes: The closing verse of the chapter contains the crucial 'holy seed' (zera qodesh) promise — the stump that remains after devastation will contain the seed of future renewal. 1QIsaiah-a uses plene spelling for qodesh (with waw) but otherwise agrees with the MT.

This verse is textually complex in both traditions. The MT's final clause (zera qodesh matsavtah, 'the holy seed is its stump/pillar') is identical in meaning in 1QIsaiah-a, preserving the note of hope at the end of the devastating commission.
